coredata.dc:title: CQDs modified Co-BiOCl nanosheets with improved effective light absorption and charge separation for photocatalytic CIP degradation and NOX removal
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenxuan
Huang, Jiehong
Fu, Xionghui
Xu, Jungu
Yu, Xiang
Zhu, Yi
Zhang, Yuanming
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the Co-BiOCl/CQDs photocatalyst, with excellent degradation performance of ciprofloxacin and NOX, was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. By quantifying the effective light absorption (I abs), charge separation efficiency (ηsep) and charge injection efficiency (ηinj) of the photocatalyst, it could be obtained that Co-doping could enhance the charge separation, and CQDs could improve the effective light absorption efficiency, while the charge injection efficiency of Co-BiOCl/CQDs was similar to that of BiOCl, demonstrating that Co and CQDs could not enhance the charge injection, and strongly demonstrating that the enhancement in effective light absorption and charge separation were main factors for improving photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the photocatalytic mechanism of CIP and NOx under visible light was also proposed. This work offered a new insight for the effect of Co and CQDs in the photocatalysts of Co-doping or CQDs based.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiOCl
Carbon quantum dots
Co-doped
NOx removal
Ciprofloxacin photodegrade

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A1515010390 ); the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 21621401 ); the Open Fund of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Supramolecular Coordination Materials and Applications (No. 2020B121201005); the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education/Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices (No. 20KF-5).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) BiOCl, CoBi2 and CoBiC2 and (b) magnified view of different prepared samples.
(a) SEM image, (b) TEM image and (c, d) HRTEM images of CoBiC2, (e) EDS pattern of CoBiC2.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) Bi 4f, (c) Cl 2p and (d) O1 s of CoBiC2.
DRS of BiOCl, CoBi2, CoBiC2 and CQDs.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of CIP and (b) the corresponding kinetic constant of degradation by CoBi1, CoBi2, CoBi3 and CoBi4.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of CIP and (b) the corresponding kinetic constant of degradation by CoBiC1, CoBiC2, CoBiC3 and CoBiC4.
(a)Photocatalytic degradation of CIP and (b) the corresponding kinetic constant of CIP degradation by BiOCl, CoBi2 and CoBiC2.
Photocatalytic removal NOx activity of (a) BiOCl, CoBi2 and CoBiC2, (b) CoBi with different amount of Co-doping, (c) CoBiC with different amount of CQDs and (d) repeated activity of CoBiC2.
(a) Nyquist plots of EIS and (b) PL spectra of BiOCl, CoBi2 and CoBiC2.
Photocurrent responses in the aqueous solution (a) without K2S2O8, (b) with K2S2O8 of BiOCl, CoBi2 and CoBiC2.
Radical trapping experiments on photocatalytic CIP degradation by CoBiC2.
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic mechanism of CIP and NOx over CoBiC2.
Schematic diagram of preparation of Co-BiOCl/CQDs.

head.ce:title: CQDs modified Co-BiOCl nanosheets with improved effective light absorption and charge separation for photocatalytic CIP degradation and NOX removal

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the Co-BiOCl/CQDs photocatalyst, with excellent degradation performance of ciprofloxacin and NOX, was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. By quantifying the effective light absorption (I abs), charge separation efficiency (ηsep) and charge injection efficiency (ηinj) of the photocatalyst, it could be obtained that Co-doping could enhance the charge separation, and CQDs could improve the effective light absorption efficiency, while the charge injection efficiency of Co-BiOCl/CQDs was similar to that of BiOCl, demonstrating that Co and CQDs could not enhance the charge injection, and strongly demonstrating that the enhancement in effective light absorption and charge separation were main factors for improving photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the photocatalytic mechanism of CIP and NOx under visible light was also proposed. This work offered a new insight for the effect of Co and CQDs in the photocatalysts of Co-doping or CQDs based.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the abuse of antibiotics has become increasingly severe, which leads to destroy the ecological balance and threaten human health [1–3]. While as one of the increasingly serious environmental problems, nitrogen oxides, derived from the consumption of fossil fuels and automobile exhaust, are the main factors for causing photochemical pollution [4–6]. Thus, there is a need to develop an effective and environment-friendly technology for removing pollutants from water and air [7–9]. Photocatalysis has the advantages of making full use of the abundant sunlight, mild reaction conditions and low cost, therefore it is regarded as a potential oxidation technology to eliminate pollutants [10–13]. Among many photocatalytic materials, owing to the typical layered structure, high chemical stability and strong oxidation ability, BiOCl has attracted the increasing attention [14]. However, the wide band gap, the ultraviolet absorption range and the high recombination rate of photogenerated carriers limit its wide applications [15,16].

To solve the above problems, doping with transition metal ions is an effective method, it has been reported that BiOCl could be narrowed the band gap by doping Fe element [17], doped Zn element could broaden the solar response range of BiOI [18], the introduction of Mn2+ to BiOCl could increase its light absorption region and facilitate photoinduced carrier separation [19]. Cobalt is one of the most effective doping species, which broaden the light absorption range of BiOCl and improve the charge separation, thereby restraining the recombination of photogenerated carrier [20,21].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), like many nano-carbon materials, have good electron storage properties, therefore, the photogenerated electrons,generated by semiconductor excitation,can be quickly transferred to CQDs [22], restraining the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. Thus CQDs could enhance the photocatalytic performance [23].

Inspired by above works, we assume that the cointroduction of Co and CQDs into BiOCl can enhance the photocatalytic activities, and the contributions of Co and CQDs to the improvement of photocatalytic performance were quantitatively evaluated, which has never been reported yet.

In this work, we combined the advantages of Co-doping and CQDs to synthesize a novel photocatalyst Co-BiOCl/CQDs, which showed remarkable removal efficiency of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and NOx. It is obtained that Co can enhance charge separation efficiency and CQDs can increase the effective light absorption. Interestingly, after being incorporated with Co and CQDs, BiOCl could not show enhanced charge injection. Therefore, the charge separation efficiency and the effective light absorption were the crucial factors for improving the photocatalytic activity, but the charge injection enhancement was not crucial. Moreover, a reasonable mechanism of photodegrading CIP and NOx removal was proposed.
The particular materials used in this work are listed in Supporting Information.

CQDs were fabricated according to a reported method [24]. Co-BiOCl/CQDs was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process. The detailed synthesis process is shown in the Supporting Information, and the schematic diagram of preparation of Co-BiOCl/CQDs is shown in Scheme 1 .

According to the amount of CoCl2·6H2O added (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 mmol), the samples are recorded as CoBi1, CoBi2, CoBi3 and CoBi4, respectively. And the samples were recorded as CoBiC1, CoBiC2, CoBiC3 and CoBiC4, respectively, which added different amounts of CQDs (1, 2, 3 and 4 mL)
The detailed characterization methods are shown in Supporting Information.
The adsorption experiment in this study are provided in Supporting Information.

The photocatalytic activity of catalyst was studyed by Photocatalytic CIP degradation the concentrations of CIP by the UV–vis spectrophotometer(TU-1901). The detailed experimental and analytic process is shown in the Supporting Information. To investigate the possible mechanism of photocatalysis, photodegradation of CIP with different scavengers,including triethanolamine (TEOA), benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA), were carried out.

The photocatalytic properties of catalysts were further estimated through the photocatalytic removal of NOX at ppb levels in a continuous flow reactor at room temperature. The detailed experimental and analytic process is shown in the Supporting Information.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the phase structures of the synthesized photocatalysts were identified, the diffraction peaks of all photocatalysts were indexed to tetragonal phase of BiOCl (JCPDS No. 06-0249) [21]. Compared to BiOCl, the peaks of CoBi2 and CoBiC2 shifted towards higher scattering angles (Fig. 1b), indicating the doped Co2+ successfully substituted for Bi3+ because the smaller atomic radius of Co2+ (0.72 Å) than Bi3+ (1.17 Å) [25,26] resulted in the less lattice parameters of Co-doped samples [27]. However, the peaks of CQDs could not be found in Co-BiOCl/CQDs samples due to their low contents in composites (Fig. S1b).

The structure and morphology of CoBiC2 were obtained from SEM and TEM. CoBiC2 was composed of numerous nanosheets with diameters of about 200 nm and thickness of about 20 nm in Fig. 2 b. As shown in Fig. 2C, the discontinuous lattice spacings were attributed to crystallographic defects in CoBiC2 (white area), indicating the existence of oxygen vacancies [28,29]. From Fig. 2d, two different lattice spacings were determined to be 0.324 and 0.197 nm, which were corresponded to the (002) planes of CQDs [24] and (200) planes of BiOCl [30], respectively. In addition, the EDS result indicated that CoBiC2 contained Bi, O, Cl, C and Co elements. And the ICP tests were conducted to further verify the existence of doped Co element in CoBiC2, and the ICP result was consistent with that of EDS. It could be seen in Table S2 that the more CoCl2·6H2O was added during the preparation process, the more Co elements as-prepared samples contained.

The elemental composition and chemical states of CoBiC2 were investigated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the survey scan XPS spectrum demonstrated thatCoBiC2 contained Bi, Cl, O, C and Co elements. For Fig. 3b, the spectrum of Bi 4f, two peaks at 159.7 and 164.7 eV belonged to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, indicating the existence of Bi3+ in the crystal structure [31–34]. As shown in Fig. 3c, two peaks 198.1 eV and 199.7 eV, were indexed to the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, respectively [35]. The O 1 s core level of XPS spectra were shown in Fig. 3d. The peak 530.2 eV was assigned to the Bi–O bonding in [Bi2O2] slabs. The peaks at 531.8 and 533.1 eV were related to C = O and O-C = O due to the present of oxygen groups-containing CQDs. The other peak 532.5 eV was corresponded to oxygen vacancies in CoBiC2, which was consistent with the TEM result. In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3e), three splitting peaks,284.6 eV [23], 285.3 eV [36] and 287.3 eV [37], were related to C–C, C–H and C–O–C due to the present of oxygen groups-containing CQDs. The Co 2p spectrum consisted of two peaks at 796.2 and 781.1 eV, were associated for Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 (Fig. 3e) [27]. The two peaks with the splitting energy Δ = 15.1 eV were attributed to the Co–O bonds in CoBiC2, conforming the interaction between doped Co and O in the crystal of CoBiC2 [21].

BiOCl only absorbed ultraviolet light with the absorption edge approximately 370 nm in Fig. 4 . Compared with BiOCl, the optical absorption intensity in visible region of CoBi2 was enhanced and the absorption edge shifted red to 378 nm, which was due to the modification of the energy band structure by Co doping [27]. And the corresponding band gap value of CQDs is 1.63 eV [38,39]. More importantly, CoBiC2 exhibited best light harvesting capability among as-prepared samples, indicating CQDs were beneficial to the light absorption of photocatalysts.

The photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out under visible light. Fig. 5 a displayed the photocatalytic performances of Co-BiOCl with different doping contents of Co. After 100 min irradiation, the photocatalytic efficiency of CoBi1, CoBi2, CoBi3 and CoBi4 were 41.7, 57.7, 54.4 and 38.8%, the corresponding kinetic constants were 0.0052, 0.0082, 0.0074and 0.0048 min–1. It was clearly that photocatalytic performance of Co-BiOCl gradually enhanced with increasing Co-doping content. However, when the doping content of Co reached up to 0.3 mmol or even higher, the photocatalytic performance decreased because excessive dopant Co could act as charge recombination centers [40].

The influence of CQDs with different content on Co-BiOCl/CQDs composites was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 , the photocatalytic performances of Co-BiOCl/CQDs were in order of CoBiC2>CoBiC1>CoBiC3>CoBiC4 and the corresponding kinetic constant were 0.0114 min–1, 0.0147 min–1, 0.0091 min–1and 0.0071 min–1, respectively. CoBiC2 exhibited the best photocatalytic activity, which was ascribed to the optimal content of CQDs could accelerate the transfer of photogenerated electron and thus restrain the recombination of charge carriers [22]. But, the overloading of CQDs performed photo-shielding effect, resulting in the blockage of light to the semiconductor and consequent decrease of rate constant [41,42].

As shown in Fig. 7 , the photocatalytic efficiencies of BiOCl, CoBi2 and CoBiC2 were 17.6%, 57.5% and 79.6%, respectively. Compared with BiOCl, the photocatalytic activity of CoBi2 was improved after Co doped, with the degradation rate constant 5.9 times higher than BiOCl. While the CoBiC2 demonstrated the best photocatalytic activity and the degradation rate constant was 10.5 times than BiOCl. Compared with reported photocatalysts, CoBiC2 did have excellent photocatalytic performance towards CIP in this work (Table S3).

The photocatalytic activity of BiOCl, CoBi and CoBiC towards NOX were analyzed in a continuous air flow under visible-light irradiation, and then the activity was evaluated by the ratio of NOX concentration in C t (outlet streams) to that in C 0 (feeding streams).

The influence of Co with different content on Co-BiOCl/CQDs composites was investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 a, after 30 min irradiation, the photocatalytic NOx removal rate of CoBi1, CoBi2, CoBi3 and CoBi4 were 19.2%, 23.2%, 14.8% and 13.2%, respectively. With the increase of Co doping content, the photocatalytic performance of Co-BiOCl gradually improved. However, the photocatalytic performance reduced when the doping content of Co reached 0.3 mmol or more, since the excessive dopant Co might work as a charge carrier recombination center [40].

The effect of different contents of CQDs on Co-BiOCl/CQDs composite materials was further studied in photodegrading NOx. The photocatalytic NOx removal rate of Co-BiOCl/CQDs in order of CoBiC2(49.5%)>CoBiC3(35.7%)>CoBiC4(31.4%)>CoBiC1(30.1%) was in Fig. 8b. Obviously, CoBiC2 revealed the best photocatalytic removal rate, which was ascribed to the optimal content of CQDs, but, too much CQDs would lead to reduce the photocatalytic performance. Since CQDs has excellent electrical conductivity, loading appropriate amount of CQDs is beneficial to the transport of photogenerated electrons, inhibits the recombination of charge carriers, significantly improves the efficiency of photocatalytic NOx removal. Similarly, it is due to overloading of CQDs performed photo-shielding effect, resulting in the decrease of photocatalytic activity [41,42].

As shown in Fig. 8c, the photocatalytic NOx removal rate of photocatalyst were in order of CoBiC2(49.5%)>CoBi2(23.2%)>BiOCl(11.8%). The co-introduction of CQDs on Co-BiOCl exerts a significant promotion effect on the visible light photocatalytic performance, which was 4 times higher than that of BiOCl. Compared with reported photocatalysts, CoBiC2 did possess good photocatalytic removal activity towards NOx (Table S4).

For practical applications, the durability of a photocatalyst under irradiation should be taken into account. As shown in Fig. 8d, after three cycles, it is clearly found that the photocatalyst can maintain efficient and long-term visible light photocatalytic activity with no obvious deactivation. The XRD pattern of the used sample in Fig S2. shows its stability, because the crystal structure is the same as fresh sample (Fig. 1).

To study the effect of Co and CQDs in improving the photocatalytic performance of BiOCl, EIS and PL were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, it could be found that the arcs radii of CoBiC2 was the smallest among all samples and the PL peak intensity of CoBiC2 was the lowest, confirming that the co-introduction of Co and CQDs significantly promoted separation and transportation of photogenerated charge carriers.
To further study the specific effects of Co and CQDs in improving the photocatalytic activity, the photocurrents calculation was analyzed.

The photocurrents (I ph) depends on three factors: I abs (light harvesting rate expressed as current density), ηsep (charge separation efficiency), and ηinj (charge injection efficiency), which could be expressed as [43]:
(1)
I
p h
=
I
a b s
×
η
s e p
×
η
i n j

K2S2O8, as electron quencher, could inhibit recombination of photogenerated carrier on the catalysts surface, so the injection efficiency(ηinj) value was 100%, therefore Eq. (1) could be simplified be:
(2)
I
p h
(
K 2
S 3
O 8
)
=
I
a b s
×
η
s e p

It can be obtained from Fig. 10 a that the photocurrent density of CoBi2 and CoBiC2 with electron scavenger were 4.6 and 10.0 times than BiOCl, and the current density ratio of samples were shown as follows:
I
a b s ( C o B i 2 )
/
I
a b s ( B i O C l )
= 1.21
I
a b s ( C o B i C 2 )
/
I
a b s ( B i O C l )
= 2.10
I
a b s ( C o B i C 2 )
/
I
a b s ( C o B i 2 )
= 1.74

Calculation details were available in the supporting information. The similar current density I abs values of BiOCl and CoBi2 indicated that Co could not enhance the effective light absorption, which were consistent with the results of DRS (Fig. 4) [43]. However, the effective light absorption improvement of photocatalyst by CQDs was a crucial factor for enhancing the photocatalytic activity, it could be obtained by the result of DRS and photocurrent responses.

According to the Eq. (2), the charge separation efficiency ratio were shown as follows:
η
s e p ( C o B i 2 )
/
η
s e p ( B i O C l )
= 3.79
η
s e p ( C o B i C 2 )
/
η
s e p ( B i O C l )
= 4.37
η
s e p ( C o B i C 2 )
/
η
s e p ( C o B i 2 )
= 1.15

The ηsep of CoBi2 was significantly increased in comparison with BiOCl. Modified with CQDs, the ηsep of CoBiC2 was similar to the values of CoBi2, indicating the introduction of Co could greatly improve the charge transfer and efficiently inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers, whereas CQDs could slightly enhance the charge separation.

As shown in Fig. 10b, the photocurrent density of CoBi2 and CoBiC2 were 4.72 times and 12.9 times higher than that of BiOCl without K2S2O8 electron scavenger. A ccording to Eq. (1), because the ratios of I abs and ηsep have been obtained, the ratios of charge injection efficiency between composites and BiOCl were calculated as follows:
η
i n j ( C o B i 2 )
/
η
i n j ( B i O C l )
= 1.17
η
i n j ( C o B i C 2 )
/
η
i n j ( B i O C l )
= 1.46
η
i n j ( C o B i C 2 )
/
η
i n j ( C o B i 2 )
= 1.25

Interestingly, the similar ηinj values of BiOCl, CoBi2 and CoBiC2 indicated that the introduction of Co and CQDs could not improve the charge injection efficiency. Therefore, the charge injection improvement by Co and CQDs was not an important factor for enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

Overall, although the co-introduction of Co and CQDs could not improve the charge injection, Co made a dominant contribute to the charge separation enhancement and the main contribution of CQDs was improving the effective light absorption, leading to remarkable photocatalytic performances.

To identify the main active species in photodegradation, the trapping experiments of CoBiC2 were performed. Three radical scavengers, TEOA, BQ and IPA, were used as the quenchers of h+, ·O2 –and ·OH, respectively. Fig. 11 showed that the photocatalytic efficiency of CoBiC2 decreased significantly, demonstrating that h+, ·O2 − and ·OH were the main active specie during photocatalytic process.

The E VB and the E CB of CoBiC2 were calculated to be 3.01 and 0.16 eV [44] through electrochemical Mott-Schottky technology [45] and XPS VB spectra [46,47], it could be seen from Figs. S5 and 6. The EVB and the ECB of CQDs were calculated to be 2.65 and 1.03 eV (calculation details were available in the supporting information). And the TEM and XPS results proved the existence of oxygen vacancies in CoBiC2, which could form the oxygen vacancies states. According to the above analysis, it was reasonable to propose the photocatalytic mechanism (Fig. 12 ). Under visible light irradiation, the electrons in the oxygen vacancies states were excited up to a reformed potential higher than the E CB and reacted with O2 to form ·O2 –. Simultaneously, the holes (h+) in the oxygen vacancies states oxidized H2O to ·OH. Thus, ·O2 –, ·OH and h+ served as active species to degrade CIP in the photocatalytic process.

The above-mentioned free radicals also participate in the process of NOx removal as active species. According to some previous reports, the following reaction mechanism (Fig. 12) can be obtained from the experiment of the free radical capture and IC (Fig. S3):
Photoexcited reaction: Eq. (1)
Co-BiOCl/CQDs + hν → h+ + e− (1)

Reactions on the conduction band: Eqs. (2)–(4)
(2) e− + O2 → ·O2 –
(3) 2NO + O2 →2NO2
(4) NO + ·O2 – → NO3 −
Reactions on the valence band: Eqs. (5)–(8)
(5) H2O + h+ → ·OH + H+

In Eq. (5), H2O comes from the catalyst adsorbing water vapor in the air.
(6) NO + h+ + H2O → NO2 − + 2H+
(7) NO + 2·OH → NO2 + H2O
(8) NO2 +2·OH→ NO3 − + H+

In this paper, we not only synthesized a novel photocatalyst Co-BiOCl/CQDs with remarkable performance of photodegrading CIP and NOx, but also quantitatively evaluated the specific roles of Co and CQDs to the improvement of photocatalytic performance. It is known from experiments and calculations, that the charge injection efficiency of obtained photocatalysts were almost the same, demonstrating Co and CQDs could not improve the charge injection. However, the the photon absorption rate and charge separation efficiency of Co-BiOCl/CQDs were significantly improved, with Co enhancing charge separation and CQDs improving the effective light absorption. This work showed the potential application of Co-BiOCl/CQDs in photodegrading pollutants in air and water under visible light, and offered a new insight for designing Co or CQDs based photocatalyst in improving photocatalytic performance.

Wenxuan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jiehong Huang: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xionghui Fu: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Resources. Jungu Xu: Funding acquisition. Xiang Yu: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Resources. Yi Zhu: Supervision, Resources, Visualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yuanming Zhang: Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A1515010390); the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 21621401); the Open Fund of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Supramolecular Coordination Materials and Applications (No. 2020B121201005); the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education/Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices (No. 20KF-5).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2021.101541.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic pollution in surface fresh waters: Occurrence and effects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.C. Danner, A. Robertson, V. Behrends, J. Reiss, Antibiotic pollution in surface fresh waters: Occurrence and effects, Sci. Total Environ.664 (2019) 793–804.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occurrence of antibiotics and antibiotic resistance genes in a typical estuary aquaculture region of Hangzhou Bay, China

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yuan, M. Ni, M. Liu, Y. Zheng, Z. Gu, Occurrence of antibiotics and antibiotic resistance genes in a typical estuary aquaculture region of Hangzhou Bay, China, Mar. Pollut. Bull.138 (2019) 376–384.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible light-assisted peroxydisulfate activation via hollow copper tungstate spheres for removal of antibiotic sulfamethoxazole

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, J. He, Y. Chen, P.Y. Liao, Z.Q. Liu, M. Zhu, Visible light-assisted peroxydisulfate activation via hollow copper tungstate spheres for removal of antibiotic sulfamethoxazole, Chin. Chem. Lett.31 (2020) 2721–2724.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic Nitrogen Oxide Removal Activity Improved Step-by-Step through Serial Multistep Cu Modifications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhao, G. Dong, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, Y. Huang, Photocatalytic Nitrogen Oxide Removal Activity Improved Step-by-Step through Serial Multistep Cu Modifications, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces11 (2019) 10042–10051.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strontium-Doped Perovskites Rival Platinum Catalysts for Treating NOx in Simulated Diesel Exhaust

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver embedded in defective twin brush-like ZnO for efficient and stable photocatalytic NO removal
